title: Appendix B — Draft: Results
text:
C I am gonna add some sources to information about the shark magnitude needed later
These parts above are me filtering the data and making sure there are only 1 row per actual tornado, and how I am excluding rows that have a 0 for their ending latitude or longitude, as well as justifying why I am okay to do that. Then I am transforming the latitude and longitude into actual math ready quantities and defining the coastline. I believe this would go in a methods section so I am not going to write on it yet for this submission.
A table summarizing the data and their possible values is below:
To examine the potential risk of a sharknado, we compute the probability of such an event occurring. From the perspective of any tornado occurring in the USA, this probability can be expressed as the product of several individual probabilities: \[
P(\text{sharknado}) =
P(\text{shark is present}) \times
P(\text{tornado is coastal to inland}) \times
P(\text{tornado is strong enough to lift a shark})
\] Alternatively, if we focus specifically on tornadoes that are coastal-to-inland, the probability of a sharknado occurring conditional on a tornado being coastal to inland can be expressed as:
\[
P(\text{sharknado | tornado is coastal to inland}) =
P(\text{shark is present}) \times
P(\text{tornado is strong enough to lift a shark | tornado is coastal to inland})
\]
The latter case is likely the better answer to our question, as we are worried about the possibility of sharknados occurring when there is a coastal to inland tornado, not when there is a tornado anywhere. As such, the following analysis will focus on the latter case.
To obtain a very conservative upper estimate of this probability, we assume that the probability a shark is present is 100%. In other words, we assume that whenever a coastal to inland tornado occurs, a shark is present near the coast and available to be lifted. While this assumption is not necessarily realistic, it provides a conservative upper bound on the probability of a sharknado. This will strengthen the evidence when the probability of a sharknado is still quite low.
To start, we must classify each tornado as being coastal to inland, inland to coastal, or neither. This could be done as just coastal to inland or not coastal to inland, but for thoroughness, we included another group for inland to coastal as well to compare.
The threshold used to decide upon “coastal to inland” was if the starting distance of the tornado was within 10000 meters of the closest coast and the ending distance of the tornado was further from the closest coast than the starting distance was. Likewise, tornadoes were classified into the “inland to coastal” group if they were more than 10000 meters from the closest coast and the ending distance of the tornado was closer to the coast than the starting distance.
Now we need to compute the probabilities for each tornado type that we have classified. This can be done quite simply by dividing the number of tornadoes that we have classified to each tornado type by the total number of tornadoes. The results are summarized in the following table:
(Note: probabilities do not exactly sum to 100% due to rounding)
As shown, there are far fewer coastal-to-inland tornadoes compared with inland-to-coastal and other tornadoes. This relatively small amount reflects the strict requirement that the tornado must start within a short distance of the coast to be classified in this category.
Lastly, we must compute the probability the tornado is strong enough to lift a shark given that the tornado is coastal to inland. [enter source here later] suggests that at least a magnitude 4 tornado would be needed so we will use a magnitude of 3 as a conservative threshold. To compute this probability, we first have to define the number of tornadoes for each combination of magnitude \(m\) and movement type \(t\), call this \(N_{m,t}\). The probability of a tornado having magnitude \(m\) given movement type \(t\) is the number of tornadoes with magnitude \(m\) and movement type \(t\) divided by the total number of tornadoes with movement type \(t\).
\[
P(\text{Magnitude} = m|\text{Movement} = t) = \frac{N_{m,t}}{\sum_{m} N_{m,t}}
\] For our specific probability we are interested in this probability where m = 4 or 5, and t = coastal to inland.
Computing this probability we get: \[
\frac{1 + 0}{1107} \approx 0.09\text{%}
\quad
(1 \text{ from magnitude 4},\ 0 \text{ from magnitude 5})
\]
It is important to note that even this very low probability is a conservative upper estimate. We assume that a shark is present along the coast whenever a coastal-to-inland tornado occurs and that a magnitude 4 tornado could lift a shark from the ocean onto land. These assumptions strongly favor the occurrence of a sharknado, since magnitude 4 tornadoes have only been observed lifting cars (objects of similar weight to sharks) several feet off the ground. [enter a source here later probably for some of this information]

title: Appendix A — Draft: Data Documentation
text:
“Storm Prediction Center Maps, Graphics, and Data Page” (n.d.)


library(tidyverse)

tornado_full_data <- read.csv("1950-2024_actual_tornadoes.csv")

tornado_paths <- tornado_full_data |>
  distinct(slat, slon, elat, elon, .keep_all = TRUE)









Tornado Data Variable Descriptions


Variable Name
Description
Type
Possible Values




yr
Year Tornado Occurred
Integer
1950-2024


date
Exact Date Tornado Occurred
Date
01-01-1950 to 12/31/2024


st
State Abbreviation Where Tornado First Touched Down
Categorical
Any US State


mag
EF Scale Magnitude of the Tornado
Integer
0-5 or -9 Meaning Unknown


slat
Latitude Where the Tornado First Touched Down
Numeric
US Latitudes ~ 29 to 50


slon
Longitude Where the Tornado First Touched Down
Numeric
US Longitudes ~ -125 to -70


elat
Latitude Where the Tornado Ended
Numeric
US Latitudes ~ 29 to 50


elon
Longitude Where the Tornado Ended
Numeric
US Longitudes ~ -125 to -70


len
Tornado Path Length (Miles)
Numeric
0-250 Miles


wid
Tornado Path Width (Yards)
Numeric
0-3500 Yards







The data were collected by NOAA National Weather Prediction Service.
The data was collected simply for the purpose of historical records and public safety, so that there can be research into these tornadoes.
The data is about tornadoes, showing where they occur and how they travel.
The data was collected from 1950-2024, and shows the date each tornado occurred as a column in the dataset.
The data was collected all around the United States. This includes (potentially) all states.
Reports from storm spotters and what the NWS considers reputable sources are submitted to the Storm Data publication by National Weather Service field offices.
The data is a simple CSV file with each row representing one tornado and each column being a different descriptor of the tornado.
The NOAA is a reputable known source for these types of data so there should be no data quality issues.
Some suggested data analysis methods include probability estimation based on magnitude and/or distance travelled inland or towards the coast
https://www.spc.noaa.gov/wcm/data/SPC_severe_database_description.pdf
https://data.cnra.ca.gov/dataset/shark-incident-database-california






Note



You should reference these documents/web pages formally using Zotero/bibtex,








Note



How many of the important values you would want to use are missing? Is there any relationship between missingness? e.g. smaller tornadoes might be less likely to have observed values. Is there a difference in which storms are recorded over time? (e.g. at some point NOAA changed and allowed tornadoes that were radar indicated to be recorded alongside those which were witnessed.)



shark_attacks <- read.csv("SharkAttacks.csv") |>
  select(IncidentNum, Date, County, Location, Species, Longitude, Latitude, Comment)

shark_attacks <- shark_attacks |>
  select(IncidentNum, Date, County, Location, Species, Longitude, Latitude, Comment)

unique(shark_attacks$Species)

 [1] "White"        "Hammerhead"   "Blue"         "Killer Whale" "Unknown"     
 [6] "blue"         "Blue*"        "Mako"         "Leopard"      "Sevengill"   
[11] "Salmon"       "Thresher"     ""            

shark_attacks_not_sharks <- shark_attacks |>
  filter(!Species %in% c("White", "Hammerhead", "Blue", "blue", "Blue", "Blue*", "Leopard", "Thresher", "Salmon", "Sevengill"))

shark_attacks_actual_sharks <- shark_attacks |>
  filter(!Species %in% c("Killer Whale", "Unknown"))

unique(shark_attacks_actual_sharks$Species)

 [1] "White"      "Hammerhead" "Blue"       "blue"       "Blue*"     
 [6] "Mako"       "Leopard"    "Sevengill"  "Salmon"     "Thresher"  
[11] ""          

view(shark_attacks_actual_sharks)





“Storm Prediction Center Maps, Graphics, and Data Page.” n.d. Accessed February 23, 2026. https://www.spc.noaa.gov/wcm/#data.

title: Appendix C — Draft: Intro/Conclusions
text:
D Introduction
Extreme weather events have long piqued public and scientific interest, especially when their consequences can be extremely dangerous for human life. Among these, the concept of a “sharknado,” a tornado capable of lifting sharks from the ocean and throwing them inland, represents an exaggerated but intriguing scenario. While such an event is widely seen as fictional, evaluating a sharknado’s plausibility provides an opportunity to apply probabilistic reasoning within real-world constraints.
This project aims to estimate the probability of a sharknado occurring by breaking up the phenomenon into a sequence of events that need to occur. In the methods section, we outline the sequence of these probabilities. These include the probability of a tornado forming near a coastal region and moving inward, the probability that such a tornado reaches a sufficient intensity to pick up and move a shark, and the assumption that a shark is present at the exact location required. To ensure a conservative upper estimate for this probability, several assumptions are intentionally biased in favor of the event occuring, such as a constant availability of sharks along coastlines and the ability for high magnitude earthquakes to pick them up and transport them inland.





flowchart LR
    A(Tornado forms near coast) --> B(Tornado moves inland)
    B --> C(Tornado strong enough to lift shark)
    C --> D(Shark present at location)
    D --> E((SHARKNADO))






Using a combination of probabilistic reasoning and simplifying assumptions, the analysis synthesizes these individual components into an overall estimate for the probability of the occurence of a sharknado. These results provide insight not only into the probability of a sharknado, but also into how compounding rare events together quickly diminishes the overall probability.


E Conclusion
The analysis demonstrates that even under highly favorable assumptions, the probability of a sharknado occurring remains extremely low. By breaking down the event into a series of necessary events, each with a very small likelihood, the combined probability becomes effectively zero. Notably, the assumption that sharks are always available along the coastline and that tornadoes of sufficient magnitude can lift them inland represents a significant overestimation, reinforcing that the calculated probability serves as a conservative upper estimate, rather than a realistic expectation.
This extremely low probability is to be expected as we are forcing many rare events to occur simultaneously. When this happens, the overall probability decreases dramatically. This idea extends beyond the concept of sharknadoes and is relevant for other concerns where many rare events need to occur together. In these scenarios, the overall likelihood will drop dramatically as the sharknado probability did.
Future work could build upon this analysis by incorporating more realistic environmental constraints, such as the distribution of shark locations and the physics-based modeling of lifting forces required for moving sharks. Ultimately, while sharnadoes are far from realistic, the analytical approach used in this study shows the power of probabilistic reasoning in exploring even the most unusual problems.
